title: What are my rights as an OFW in Hong Kong?

messages.content: What are my rights as an OFW in Hong Kong?

triage_result.Original Input: What are my rights as an OFW in Hong Kong?
triage_result.Normalized English: What are the rights of an Overseas Filipino Worker in Hong Kong?

Search Signals: Overseas Filipino Worker rights Hong Kong
Filipino worker legal protections Hong Kong
Labor laws for Filipinos in Hong Kong

metadata.title: . Ban on direct-hiring. No employer may hire a Filipino worker for overseas employment except
metadata.parent_context:
. Ban on direct-hiring. No employer may hire a Filipino worker for overseas employment except 
through the Boards and entities authorized by the Secretary of Labor. Direct-hiring by members of the 
diplomatic corps, international organizations and such other employers as may be allowed by the Secretary of 
Labor is exempted from this provision.

metadata.title: . Citizenship requirement. Only Filipino citizens or corporations, partnerships or entities at least
metadata.parent_context:
. Citizenship requirement. Only Filipino citizens or corporations, partnerships or entities at least 
seventy-five percent (75%) of the authorized and voting capital stock of which is owned and controlled by 
Filipino citizens shall be permitted to participate in the recruitment and placement of workers, locally or 
overseas.

metadata.title: . Foreign employment. The Commission shall ensure adequate coverage of Filipino employees
metadata.parent_context:
. Foreign employment. The Commission shall ensure adequate coverage of Filipino employees 
employed abroad, subject to regulations as it may prescribe.

metadata.title: Documentary requirements relating to paternity leave pay:
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
birth in Hong Kong
	
(1)	
For section 15H(b), an employee who takes paternity leave in 
respect of the birth of a child in Hong Kong must provide the 
employer with the birth certificate of the child—
	
(a)	
that is issued under the Births and Deaths Registration 
Ordinance (Cap. 174); and
	
(b)	
on which the employee’s name is entered as the child’s 
father.
	
(2)	
Despite subsection (1), if the child is born dead, or if the 
child dies after birth and no birth certificate has been issued in 
respect of the child under the Births and Deaths Registration 
Ordinance (Cap. 174), the employee must provide the 
employer with—
	
(a)	
a medical certificate described in subsection (3); and
	
(b)	
(if the employer so requires) a written statement signed 
by the employee, stating that—
Last updated date
11.12.2020

3A-12

(i)	
he is the father of the child delivered by the 
woman named in the medical certificate; and
	
(ii)	
the child is born dead or dies after birth (whichever 
is appropriate).
	
(3)	
For subsection (2)(a), the medical certificate—
	
(a)	
must certify the delivery of the child; and
	
(b)	
must be issued by—
	
(i)	
a registered medical practitioner; or
	
(ii)	
despite section 16 of the Midwives Registration 
Ordinance (Cap. 162), a midwife registered under 
section 8, or deemed to be registered under section 
25, of that Ordinance.
	
(4)	
The documents required under this section must be provided 
to the employer—
	
(a)	
within 12 months after the first day on which the 
employee takes the paternity leave; or
	
(b)	
if the employee has ceased to be employed by the 
employer, within the period mentioned in paragraph (a) 
or within 6 months after the cessation (whichever period 
expires first).

messages.llm_response_preview:
It is completely natural to want to understand your rights and protections while working far from home. Knowing what you are entitled to can provide a sense of security and help you feel more empowered in your workplace.

**Simplified Legal Concept**
As an OFW in Hong Kong, your rights are protected

messages.content:
It is completely natural to want to understand your rights and protections while working far from home. Knowing what you are entitled to can provide a sense of security and help you feel more empowered in your workplace.

**Simplified Legal Concept**
As an OFW in Hong Kong, your rights are protected by two different sets of laws: **Philippine Laws** (which protect you during the recruitment process and ensure your welfare as a citizen) and **Hong Kong Laws** (which govern your daily working conditions, such as your contract, pay, and leaves once you are physically in Hong Kong).

**General Explanation**
To make it easier to understand, we can look at your rights in two stages:

**1. Your Rights under Philippine Law (Before and during deployment):**
*   **Protection from Illegal Recruitment:** You have the right to be hired through authorized agencies. The law generally prohibits "direct hiring," meaning your employer should go through official channels like the Department of Migrant Workers (DMW) to ensure you are protected.
*   **Right to Proper Recruitment:** Only agencies that are Filipino-owned (at least 75% Filipino-owned) are allowed to recruit you, ensuring that your agency is accountable to Philippine standards.
*   **Right to Government Support:** The Philippine government has a duty to ensure you have adequate coverage and support while you are working abroad.

**2. Your Rights under Hong Kong Law (While working in HK):**
*   **Employment Ordinance Protections:** Once you are in Hong Kong, the local *Employment Ordinance* protects you. This includes rights to specific benefits, such as paternity leave for fathers (provided you provide the necessary birth certificates or medical documents).
*   **Record Keeping:** Employment agencies in Hong Kong are required by law to keep accurate records of your employment, including your name, passport number, and the details of your employer. This ensures there is a paper trail of your employment.
*   **Standard Working Conditions:** You are entitled to the terms stated in your employment contract, which must follow Hong Kong's labor standards regarding wages, rest days, and holidays.

In summary, Philippine laws act as your shield during the hiring process, while Hong Kong laws govern your actual daily life and benefits as an employee in the city.

Does this help clarify the different types of protections available to you, or would you like me to explain a specific right in more detail?

***

*Note: This information is provided as general guidance and may not reflect the most current legal updates. For specific legal concerns or if you are experiencing issues with your employer or agency, please contact:*

*   **Department of Migrant Workers (DMW):** For issues regarding recruitment, contracts, or agency disputes.
*   **Overseas Workers Welfare Administration (OWWA):** For welfare assistance and support services.
